comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Joséphin Péladan (1858-1918), the extraordinary Rosicrucian art critic and magus of the Decadent movement, whose masterwork How to Become a Mage will be out next year in a new English translation. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Please note that this Magic Monday will be closing early because I want to celebrate New Year's Eve without having to hover over this journal. Any question received by 10 pm Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. Have a happy New Year celebration, and see you next week!***
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Happy New Year!

I have quite a few questions, so here goes.

1) You mentioned earlier that you think that it takes about thirty years out of incarnation to be able to process the experiences of the last incarnation, and offered gender dysphoria as one possible issue that occurs if you are reincarnated too soon.

This raises a few questions:

a) Do you think that having bits of the last personality superimposed on the new one is linked to the massive increase in mental illness in the present?

b) What other issues could arise? I keep thinking I'm much shorter than I really am, so is it possible that's because my last life was short?

c) You also said you suspected that a lot of souls will spend millennia out of incarnation once populations go down to normal levels again. Which souls would go out of incarnation?

d) You've also said that a lot of large animal souls are being incarnated as human due to a lack of large animals. Why wouldn't they sit out for a little while? Is there some sort of urge to incarnate in anything even remotely suitable?

2) You also mentioned collective karma last week, and I have a question about that. Is there any way to avoid being hit by it? I'd assume a very good place to start would be identifying how I personally benefit from whatever injustices I see, and then work to reduce the benefits, but is there anything else I can do?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1a) that's a hypothesis that would be worth exploring.

1b) good question. Remember that nearly all of traditional occult philosophy dates from a time when the world's population was a small fraction of what it is today -- we didn't hit two billion until 1927, and we're at more than four times that now. So it's not surprising that we're still scrambling to try to figure out what's happening and why.

1c) nearly all of them. The only people who will be going back into incarnation right away are those who have serious karma to work off or those who have some other important reason to enter incarnation. That's one of the reasons you get such a steep drop in population as a civilization falls.

1d) if an animal soul needs an incarnation, and is fairly close to the human level anyway, it's going to take a convenient human body if that's what's available.

2) Refuse to share in the benefits, even when this is a personal disadvantage to you, and you make it more likely that you won't be part of the body count. It's not certain -- your karma may still involve dying in a natural disaster, say -- but it helps.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1b) Of course, by the time we have even started to figure it out populations will have plummeted enough it won't matter, so it's probably not worth worrying about it too much.

1c) So, would it be a good idea to try to avoid picking up karma right now so that when the crash happens, I'll have enough time out of incarnation to process it properly? If so, how would I go about that?

1c1) What other important reasons would there be to enter incarnation? The only one I can think of is a soul might do it to finish reaching Gwynfudd, but are there others?

1c2) This would also seem to imply that time out of incarnation gets scrunched due to high populations regularly. Is it possible that part of the way civilizations ossify is that the souls involved don't spend enough time out of incarnation to be able to approach things with fresh insights and get stuck rehashing things?

1d) Might all these new humans explain why spirituality is so rare these days? If I understand it correctly the first few lifetimes are usually devoted to learning to be human, and spirituality usually comes later. So, if a very large number of souls are new, then it would make sense for them to be nonspiritual. This might even be more important for those that are not quite ready to be human.

2) I'm quite a ways away from being able to do that! Better start working on learning to get by with a low enough energy/resource base I can avoid the worst of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1c) Yes, and you should also try to work off as much of your existing burden as you can so that won't drag you back. As for how to do it, well, there's this field of study called "ethics," you know, which has a lot to say about that!

1c1) If you owe a karmic debt to someone who's going to be going into incarnation, or if your destiny (the direction in which your higher self is moving) can express itself most effectively in a specific set of conditions present in the decline.

1c2) That's another hypothesis worth exploring.

1d) exactly. If you haven't been human before, going beyond the human level (which is what spirituality is about) isn't on your radar screen yet; your job is to get to know the human level and experience what it has to teach you. Brand new humans also don't generally get far with the subtler and more refined aspects of human culture, which explains a lot about the arts and music these days.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is there any way to burn off a karmic debt now? As unpleasant as it would be do it here and now, this life is in good shape, and I'm very ready for a break from being human.

Coming back a few centuries from now (or more) sounds appealing, but I'd like to avoid the post-industrial dark ages.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, you can burn off a karmic debt now, but that usually involves having your life really, thoroughly suck for a good long time. Are you up for that?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes. Is there a ritual or something I can do to bring it on myself today, or do I just need to make right with anyone I wronged?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you wish to do this, resolve firmly within yourself to accept all the consequences of your negative karma in this life, realizing that once you do the ritual that follows, there's no way back. Then, at midnight on seven successive nights, someplace where you are completely alone, say aloud seven times, "Lords of Karma, I accept all that I have done in this life and all previous lives, and I call upon myself, in this life, all the consequences of all my past actions."

Do not do this unless you are really, really, really sure you're willing to take the consequences. Those could very possibly involve something like being thrown in prison for a crime you didn't commit, and left there until you die; or coming down with a slow, untreatable, fatal, and hideously painful disease; or having every relationship you care about blow up in your face, and everyone you know is certain that it's your fault; or what have you. You are playing not merely with fire but with thunderbolts here. I know people who've done this, and come through the other side right side up and smiling after a certain number of years of living in Hell -- but that's not guaranteed at all.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The potential complexity of Karma seems dizzying:
https://studybuddhism.com/en/advanced-studies/lam-rim/karma-advanced/mechanism-of-karma-gelug-prasangika/karmic-potentials-tendencies-and-constant-habits

If changing consciousness in accordance with will, is there an expected outcome (i.e what is the purpose of such a working?). Without a clear intention the scope for unintended consequences seems high?

If Karma is not as simple as merit and debt, but is an accumulation of patterns, habits and tendencies (e.g. if I carry on this way I will likely end up where I'm heading), does the idea 'I'm very ready for a break from being human' serve as a strong enough focus to effect a radical change in perceiving reality through karmic filters?

Might lighting a fuse like that be akin to the blowback that can come from raising the kundalini when that goes pear-shaped?

If we were to choose to change perception by altering the view of reality we have through karmic filters by causing a massive shift (if that makes any sense?), what replaces the tendencies we have and how might it work that paying off karmic debt in a single life results in "I'm very ready for a break from being human"?

I can see that it might be one possibility - and call me over-cautious and a 'scaredy-cat' but is this not like paddling a canoe out to sea to visit a hurricane?

my grasp of the idea of karma is a bit thin!

replies.title: PS on that last
replies.content:
PS from the link on karma:

"To purify negative potentials does not mean that they are eradicated or removed from our mental continuum. They are just made to undergo a phase transition. They are still there and are still imputable but because we still have karmic tendencies, in the future we could repeat the negative actions that we did in the past. It doesn’t mean that you’re never going to do them again. That’s how it works."

Seems to imply that shifting karmic potentials does not mean one will not find themselves in the same place again because the underlying patterns (karmic tendencies) have not been resolved?

i.e. just because one 'pays off' a karmic debt, if the potentials/patterns have not been addressed in a person, and the same tendencies allowed to remain and drive the direction of one's path, nothing has really changed?

So, one might pay off 'debt', but paying off debt doesn't necessarily change who one is, or where headed?

Then again, perhaps working it off in one hit could work if you knew what had to be considered before initiating the process...

And then again, perhaps such an accelerated process is already unfolding around us and is why the world seems so mashed. We're not so much visiting a hurricane as living through a karmic-raspberry-jam shale-storm!

[earthworm]

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Your thoughts on reincarnation have been wonderful to contemplate and integrate into my thoughts. This comment has me thinking of how spiritual and god-like certain animals seem to me, particularly hawks and eagles. But yes, I understand the struggle it must be to be a hawk in search of its next meal. But I definitely don’t look at most humans that way....spiritual and god-like. Do you ever get conflicted thoughts about the human realm being the last stop of physical life on this planet? Happy New Year JMG!
Mike T

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
We're so clumsy because we're making the first tentative moves into a new mode of being. By the time a soul becomes a hawk, say, it's had vast amounts of experience being an animal, and so what you're seeing in a hawk's flight is the perfect grace that comes from doing something you know inside and out, right down to the marrow of your bones.

What you're seeing when you look at human beings, by contrast, is very much like watching a very young child try to figure out how to walk. Yeah, it really looks oafish, because we're in the first clumsy stages of learning.

replies.title: Reincarnation
replies.content:
Some comments from the Michael Teaching (which does not agree with traditional western occultism on reincarnation, so be warned).

It does take a substantial amount of whatever the Astral Plane uses for time to process out the incarnation so the next one can be “clean.” However, that doesn’t translate 1 to 1 to physical plane time. The amount of overpopulation is causing all kinds of havoc with the usual assumptions about time sequences in incarnation.

1a) Probably not - there are a lot of good, mundane explanations.

1b) That specific example: probably not.

1c. That makes no sense in the MT framework. A couple of the base assumptions are simply too different. There are only about 2 billion Essences, and a lot of them have either cycled off or not started incarnating yet. The rest of the population is supported by having multiple incarnations in the same time frame. What will happen is that the number of bodies needed for multiple incarnations in the same time frame will drop off and eventually disappear.

1d. The MT doesn’t agree that animal souls can migrate to human bodies. There are other explanations for the evidence that supports this idea.

2. Collective karma has to be balanced by the collective. If you were part of the collective that created the karma, you will be part of the collective that balances it. That depends, of course, on whether your Essence (High Self, etc.) wants the experience. It may not. Lots of people who appeared to be part of the collective on an issue were essentially bystanders: they were incarnate at the time, and they did the best they could with the situation.

That doesn’t mean that efforts to understand and process the karma now, in this lifetime, are futile. They can be highly productive and, in the best case, eliminate the necessity to schedule resolving it into another lifetime.

John Roth

comments.title: Metempsychosis
comments.content: Is exhaustion with metempsychosis coupled with a desire for ascension into the divine a healthy sign?

replies.title: Re: Metempsychosis
replies.content: It really does vary. In some cases, yes, it's an indication that you have a call to the mystical path; in other cases, it's an attempt to run away from life, or more specifically from some kind of karmic debt you don't want to deal with. How do you tell? Lots of prayer -- specifically, the kind that involves listening to the divine rather than talking to it -- is generally a good start.

replies.title: Re: Metempsychosis
replies.content: What about a sense of wanting a break? Not necessarily not wanting to be here, but more a sense of wanting to spend some time away from being human?

replies.title: Re: Metempsychosis
replies.content: That's quite reasonable -- since incarnations are crowded up close to one another these days, I think a lot of people are starting to feel good and ready for a long, long stay in the after-death state.

replies.title: Re: Metempsychosis
replies.content: Do you think an intense disinterest in the present, but intense curiosity and interest in the far future (a couple thousand years from now) could be a form that could take?

replies.title: Re: Metempsychosis
replies.content: It's hard to tell, as there can be many causes for such an emotional state.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a bit of an odd question: I'm involved in a role playing group, and while most of the time it feels like I'm creating something, there's one NPC who stands out. His name is Chirp (a name given to him by the party since his name is real name is hard to pronounce), a rat-spirit. He's immensely powerful, and has a very warped sense of humour.

Most of the time, with NPCs, I can control them. I know their basic personalities, goals, hopes, dreams. I play them. With Chirp, he comes when he pleases, and does what he wants. Half the time he's aiming for something I never even thought of until suddenly I figure it out, and sometimes I just have no clue what he's doing.

Do you have any idea what Chirp could be?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The universe is full of different kinds of entities, some of which are well known to occultists, most of which are not. "The macrocosmic universe is one vast infinite sphere," says one of the Golden Dawn knowledge lectures, "containing so many and diverse infinite microcosmic forms, of which the perfect knowledge is known only to the advanced adept" -- and no, I'm nothing like that advanced!

So no, I don't have any definite idea. Have you asked him?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have indeed, and his answer is "I am what I am". It is very vague, and unhelpful. While I'm curious, it seems very clear that he's not interested in giving me definite answers, so I thought I'd ask here.

Ultimately I don't care too much what he is: he's made the games much more enjoyable. I'm still quite curious though, and if I could figure it out I would love to know!

I'm nowhere near advanced enough to know though, and I suppose I should just accept it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Does he seem to have any capacity to influence physical reality?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This sort of reminds me of the Philip experiment in the ‘70’s.  For those who haven’t heard of it, some researchers dreamed up a life story of a nobleman, then tried to contact him in seances as if he were a real ghost, to see if he’d respond.  After a while, he did.  I have never bern able to find a thorough account so I don’t know if he continued to “ exist” after the experiment stopped.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Check this link:

https://www.google.com/search?tbm=bks&q=%22members+reminded+philip+playfully%22

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That is a very good question. I don't think so, but I'm not sure.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This sounds like a version of the Trickster. Trickster can assume many shapes; Coyote, Raven, Hare, Spider, to name a few. I've never heard of him taking a rat form but given that he's a shape-shifter, he can no doubt take on any form that pleases him. As to what end, well he does tend to usher in discord or change in many stories. I believe John has posted several essays about Trickster's role over on Ecosophia.

JLfromNH

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This has happened to me on occasion, also in RP, and was especially prominent in 2015 when my progressed Sun was conjunct my natal Neptune - my characters seemed to have minds of their own, and I was unable to explain their behaviors or motivations to the DM or to the rest of the party because they just did what they wanted and only answered questions reluctantly.

Is there anything notable happening astrologically in respect to your natal Neptune by transit or progression right now?

comments.title: Occulted book launches, oh my!
comments.content:
Rumor has it you have a new book on conspiracies out.

How does this compare to your earlier encyclopedia on the topic (which sinister forces have erased from your website's bibliography)?

In any event, have a good New Year!

replies.title: Re: Occulted book launches, oh my!
replies.content:
Oh, do you mean this one? ;-)

[IMAGE:0]

Like my earlier title from Sterling, The Occult Book, it's a set of 100 vignettes from history -- in this case, the history of secret societies and conspiracies -- with gorgeous illustrations. It's not an encyclopedia; it's more of a coffee table book, really, or an easy introduction to the history of the subject.

As for the biblio on the website, yeah, I know. I'm hoping to make time to get that up to date in January.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Are there any occult influences from eating fermented foods? They are usually crawling with living things, so I'd imagine those would have some impacts, but I'm unsure of what exactly they would be.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: All living things feed on other living things. That's true even of plants, which send their roots in search of the flesh of dead plants and animals, and it's certainly true of you and me. Eating yogurt or kimchi or miso or what have you is no different from eating anything else; as an old ritual has it, "all things have died that you might live."

replies.title: Hang on a minute...
replies.content:
I hate to be That Person, but there really is a difference between autotrophy and heterotrophy. Most plants don't have to obtain organic compounds from soil, which is why they can be grown hydroponically unless they require living bacterial or fungal symbionts. The few parasitic plants e.g. Orobanchaceae, attach their roots to those of living plants, not dead plants.

But this does not explain the weirdly fattened trunks and pulpy fruit of the trees at the cemetery....
-Dewey

replies.title: Re: Hang on a minute...
replies.content: Sure, under artificial conditions you can force plants to grow that way, but in a natural setting plant roots seek out concentrations of organic matter, and thrive best in soil that contains a high proportion of humus, i.e., dead things well rotted. I've wondered more than once if the oddly devitalized quality of food plants grown hydroponically is a reflection of the lack of aspects of plant nutrition not well understood by a purely reductionistic science.

replies.title: Re: Hang on a minute...
replies.content:
THis may b e relevant here:

www.scientificamerican.com/article/soil-depletion-and-nutrition-loss/

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Is there anything special Druids do on New Year’s Eve?

I was once married to a musician so I am uninterested in the secular type NYE. It’s just a longer work night with more drunks, including your husband because the band always wants to take advantage of the free champagne. So the wives end NYE by going to some remote party house at oh-dark- thirty to pick up their husbands. (One lady always said “Next year I’m gonna kill him on December 30 and hide the body.” 😄)

At any rate, I hope everyone else has a good time!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Druid New Year is November 1, so it's already come and gone here. The official new year is purely a secular thing for us, and I don't know of any Druids who do anything noticeably different from their neighbors.

Have a happy one anyway!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: As a fellow Druid, I can attest to not having seen anything to suggest it's different from any other day. Of course, I also host a New Years party, but not for any spiritual reason, but more because I take any excuse I can to host one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi, Happy New Year, and thank you!

Three questions this time:

1) There's a fairly vile woman in my LARPing circle who's amassed enough of a cult of personality that the game-runners, for financial reasons, can't just throw her out on her ear. I don't want to hurt her (well, I do, but I know doing so magically would be unwise), but I want to make her go away--and interestingly, she had been talking about maybe getting a job offer in Michigan. Would the rite where I baptize a doll as her and mail it to Hell, MI be a good idea, or would the raspberry jam involved mean I end up having to leave the community?

2) I've ended up starting on the exercises in Paths of Wisdom without reading Learning Ritual Magic first, and some back-reading this week gave me the impression that's out of order. Should I pause the former and start over with the latter?

3) Is it okay to do discursive meditation in the Paths of Wisdom model while walking, even though that's outside the banished space? I wouldn't mind getting my physical and mental exercise in at the same time. :)

Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's certainly a form that the blowback could take. You might consider doing a working to bring harmony and good feeling to the LARPing group, and let the gods decide how they want to make that happen.

2) Don't worry about it. I'd recommend studying Learning Ritual Magic before you take up the practices in Circles of Power, if you decide to do that, but Paths of Wisdom doesn't require that preparation.

3) Yes -- walking meditation is actually quite traditional. Try synchronizing your breathing with your pace in the opening phases of the practice.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I'd kinda thought that might be the case--I guess I was just hoping there was a loophole for my overdeveloped sense of vengeance. :P The alternative you suggest is more tactically sound, so I'll go with that, and thanks!

2 and 3) Excellent, thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: One bit of magical tactics to keep in mind is that it's best to aim for your actual goal, rather than specifying some means to that goal. Is your goal in the situation vengeance, or is it an improved LARPing experience? If the latter, that's what you aim for -- and of course it doesn't hurt that the blowback from giving everyone a better experience isn't something to be worried about...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent point. Vengeance would be temporarily satisfying, but probably a bigger-picture view is called for here.

comments.title: Two Divination Systems at Once
comments.content:
Happy New Year JMG!

First of all, many thanks for your extensive answer on "Druidry Flavors" last week. Your description of the AODA was entertaining and evocative!

A couple questions:

The first relates to divination. I have been doing daily divinations with geomancy as outlined in your book "The Art and Practice of Geomancy" for a few months now, with very good results. I am now exploring the practices as outlined in the Druidry Magic Handbook, which of course means using ogham. As I take up ogham, is it still possible to continue with geomancy as well? In other words, what considerations are there for using two different divination systems for the same daily divination questions? Is it best to just stick with one?

My second question relates to doing the SoP as an "inner working" -- I think that's how you phrased it when you mentioned an example of doing a ritual on the train with your eyes closed and nobody the wiser. My living circumstances allow for meditation, but not so much the standing and arm movements of the SoP. Same goes for doing the SoP in highly trafficked natural settings. Will doing the SOP in your head the majority of the time have an effect on its efficacy or are there any potential problems with doing that?

Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: Two Divination Systems at Once
replies.content:
1) I'd encourage you to use only one at a time for daily divinations, but you can certainly use more than one divination system for other questions. For example, when Ogham was my usual daily divination method, I was also casting geomancy readings for questions having to do with money and my writing career, and getting good results.

2) You won't get the same intensity of effect if you can't bring it down to the material plane most of the time, but if that's what you can do, do it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Since reincarnation has come up here, I have a second question: If that what you have explained about reincarnation in times when the human population was much lower than today is correct, then it must logically follow that in the average non-industrial society or in the average hunter-gatherer society there are far fewer proles than in industrial society today, so to speak. Does that make sense?

replies.title: Say what?!
replies.content:
Surely you mean that there were fewer bourgeoisie. :-)
-Dewey

replies.title: Re: Say what?!
replies.content: That, too, but I referred to J. M. Greers opinion that there were fewer people who were inept at living a human live back then.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: P. S.: It seems to me that "proles" isn't a very good translation of the German word "Prolet". Apologies for any misunderstandings.

comments.title: Deity contact for beginners
comments.content:
Hello JMG. So, after about 3 thousand years of research, I decided I want to start working with the Tuatha de Danann, with Brigid being my main goddess. I ordered a statue of Her which should be arriving shortly.

What method(s) would be safe for a beginner trying to contact and establish a relationship with Her? I have Courtney Weber’s Brigid book, so I have access to some pretty decent prayer material.

replies.title: Re: Deity contact for beginners
replies.content:
Daily reflective prayer is the basic practice in this kind of work. Once you have the statue, consider getting a couple of candles and something scented -- incense is fine, or you could use a potpourri of appropriate herbs and resins, which can be renewed with a blend of essential oils. Light the candles, do whatever needs doing with the scented stuff, recite a prayer you've chosen -- the same one every time, please, as it helps you attune to the practice -- and then listen inwardly, with your mind. Give that five or ten minutes either once or twice a day, and the relationship will develop from there.

(...and, yes, if you've read a certain novel of mine, the resemblance here is the opposite of accidental...)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I've been thinking of a pantheon with which to work with, and the Roman one seems to resonate with me. I was quite surprised by this, but then I realised that I live about two miles from what used to be an enormous Roman garrison. I always used to dismiss the Roman presence in Britain - I considered them to be unwelcome temporary occupiers - but I've had to reconsider this, and now think their roots might go very deep here indeed.

Anyway, I've thought about how the SoP might work with a Roman pantheon, and I've come up with this:

Air - Jupiter
Fire - Vesta
Water - Neptune
Earth - Flora
Spirit Below - Tellus
Spirit Above - Sol

Do you think this is viable? Also, would I need to find a Roman equivalent for the great word "Awen"?

Thank you very much for your consideration.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It looks perfectly sound to me. The Latin word to use in place of Awen is Fiat! -- beyond its literal meaning, it stands for Flatus, Ignis, Aqua, Terra -- air, fire, water, and earth.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have a bit of a follow up question for you related to children and magic.

Before starting any magic, my wife and I were doing the grove opening and closing weekly as laid out in the Druidry Handbook and celebrated parts of the eight fold year with the ceremonies in the Druidry Handbook. We also included our little kids (4, 7, 10) in the groves if they wanted to, and they certainly participated in the holy day ceremonies with speaking roles.

I read in your magic Monday that grove ceremonies are not recommended around kids. Is this true for the celebration of the yearly cycle? If so, how do you recommend celebrating the turning of the year with children and without grove ceremonies?

Thank you,
Matt

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Here we go again...

No, that is ***not*** what I said. You can do absolutely any practice in The Druidry Handbook around kids. What you can't do around kids are serious workings of ceremonial magic -- for example, the grove ceremony in The Celtic Golden Dawn, which is emphatically not the same as the one in The Druidry Handbook.

Apologies if I sound a little testy about this, but no matter how many times I specify that the only thing you can't do around children is serious ceremonial magic, someone reads something in a past Magic Monday, takes it out of context, and thinks that I've said you can't do any kind of ritual at all around children.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Perhaps an analogy between cooking around kids, and doing serious chemistry with strong and potentially toxic if misused substances around them, is appropriate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That would fit the situation very well, yes.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: According to Michael Winn, the Daoist monasteries in China that have been in use for thousands of years, have distinctly different chi in the surrounding areas. Adepts continuously running it through their bodies and working with the etheric energy refined it. It's presented as an unambiguously good thing, but would all the spirits of the land welcome that? If there were equivalent sites where Western occultists had been using astral energy for a similar period of time, what sort of imprint would that leave? Would long-term practice of mysticism at a particular site leave a different type of imprint as well?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For the better part of two thousand years, Western occultists had to keep a very low profile to avoid severe social and (often) legal penalties, so the rituals practiced in Western occult traditions are specifically designed not to leave such an imprint -- that's why we have opening and closing rituals at the beginning and end of our workings, the first to open a sacred space and the second to shut it down and dissipate the energies. It's only been a matter of decades since that changed, so we simply don't know -- and since many occultists are quite aware that the current climate of relative tolerance for our traditions may not last, we're not in a rush to do anything that would increase our vulnerability.

As for long-term practice of Western mysticism, it really varies. Some modes of mystical practice seem to foster that, but many others don't.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
If someone serves a god well in their life and is rewarded (with a period of time with their god's host, their version of heaven, or something else) before their next life, what kind of effect does that have when they do reincarnate?

How much scope do gods have to punish those who wrong them and their followers?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Good question. I doubt it would be any kind of disadvantage, but I don't know of anything in occult literature that discusses that.

2) It depends very much on the god.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Happy New Year JMG!

catherine yronwode in her comprehensive and highly informative _Hoodoo Herb and Root Magic_ notes, on page 9, that some of of the African Influences on Hoodoo include an emphasis on the power of the footracks, feet, shoes and stockings as well as "a seeming reverence for the number nine above other numbers."

This got me thinking; 9 is of course the number of Yesod, the Etheric plane. Most of catherine's book deals with the natural magical properties of material objects. It seems then that Hoodoo's natural magic techniques focus with great intensity on inducting the etheric charge of material substances into the realm of manifest reality. This may then in part explain why the feet are so emphasized, as the Malkuth center is located right around the feet.

Likewise, this may then help elucidate why doing a floor wash can work so amazingly effectively; it inducts the etheric charge of herbs and other substances right to the level of the feet, the level of embodied reality, likewise with herbal baths, both of which are, of course, extensively developed in the Rootwork traditions.

My basic question is: do you think I'm on the right track in how I'm theoretically categorizing these specific Hoodoo techniques, and if not, where am I missing the mark?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: A very productive meditation! Follow it out and see where else it takes you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Can giving to charities help mitigate or work out karma? If so do you have a pointer to some appropriate techniques?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It depends very much on the nature of the karma. If your karmic burden involves a major overload in the direction of Saturn, then generosity -- the keynote of Jupiter -- will indeed help balance it out, by bringing you more into balance with Jovian energies. If it's a different kind of karma -- say, something of Mars -- it won't do you much good. You basically need to figure out which planet best represents your difficult karma, and then enact the positive expression of the opposite planet -- Saturn and Jupiter, Mars and Venus, Sun and Moon are opposites, and Mercury is of course its own opposite. You'll know you've got the right thing if the positive expression of the planet you have in mind seems actively repulsive to you.

comments.title: Divination and children
comments.content:
Hey, John Michael, thanks for taking the time to do this today! And have a great time tonight. It's strange, celebrating the Solstice makes it feel as if New Year came 10 days ago to me, and it does deflate the importance of the calendar new year somewhat, but still...this is the world we live and operate in physically.

My query is this: both of my children have a Magic 8 Ball and use it regularly for divination. Both of them want a "better" oracle, more like mine. They are 8 and 10 years old. Is this too young for something like a bag of rune stones or a card deck and the associated divination literature?

Also, I know your feelings about Harry Potter, and we are always careful to distinguish Harry Potter magic from real magic, but is there anything meaningful about the age of 11 for beginning introductory magical training, like they do in the Harry Potter movies? We already do regular focus training exercises with them, and I assume that is beneficial at any age. But they are both anxious to do more.

Apologies if you've answered these before. And thank you!
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Divination and children
replies.content:
A rune set, an Ogham set, or a card deck -- the Lenormand deck is especially well suited to children, and others who don't have much exposure to cartomancy -- or some other basic oracle should be fine for children age 7 and up. Some children can become overdependent on their oracles, but then the same is true of many adults, and you should be able to guide your children to a more realistic attitude if that happens.

JK Rowling used the age of 11 for those books, iirc, because that's traditionally when children in England go to boarding schools. (The Harry Potter franchise is a mashup of modern pop fantasy with that venerable British tradition, the public-school novel.) It's not relevant to magical training. I'd encourage you to consider teaching your kids to meditate, and get them started doing simple, positive natural magic -- protection, healing, blessing, and so on. Seasonal rituals are also great for children, and they can be taught speaking parts so they begin to get a sense of how to perform in a public context -- a great source of confidence-building for when they have other things to do out there in the world. Ritual magic should wait until puberty's pretty well along, but that won't be so many years from now!

comments.title: Nature spirits
comments.content: Have you (or anybody on Magic Mondays) read and do you have an opinion on books about folklore and, almost by default, European folk magic by Claude Lecouteaux? Anything written about nature spirits by Marko Pogacnik? Some of their books look interesting, but I've noticed that it's become trendy of late to write about these subjects and I suspect that at least some authors aren't exactly authorities on what they write.

replies.title: Re: Nature spirits
replies.content: I haven't read either of these authors. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Nature spirits
replies.content:
Hmm! I just last week found an affordable copy of Pogacnik's book Nature Spirits and Elemental Beings. I've only flipped through it. He's a Slovenian artist who wrote this and at least one related book in German in the 1990s. He developed a form of earth healing he calls lithopuncture, which involves "placing stone slabs with sculpted signs...on points of the earth's geomantic organism which are equivalent to the acupuncture points of the human body." He describes direct experiences and communication with elemental beings and other consciousnesses. There's some discussion of nature temples and sacred groves. Of course I can't vouch for the author, or even recommend the book yet, but the way he's describing things doesn't seem out of touch with traditional lore, or with the sorts of conversations that happen around here.

Jonathan.

replies.title: Re: Nature spirits
replies.content:
I have more than a dozen of Lecouteaux's books, and have spent a lot of time poking around in them. He has collected a vast amount of absolutely fascinating material from Western European folk magic (including the classic folk grimoires) and magical folk lore, and he seems to present it accurately--I have checked his sources more than once. I think his books are very much worth one's time and money.

I haven't found Lecouteaux to be particularly deep in trying to understand what makes (or sometimes, fails to make) these folk practices effective and meaningful. However, that's OK. The "folk" themselves are notoriously ecclectic, and don't demand that their practices all make sense together. "If it works, good, and we'll keep on doing it, whether we can make sense of it or not. If it doesn't work, why do it again?" seems to be the main point.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John—

If, as the Kabilion states, all is Mind, why does an intellectual path not work with respect to the spiritual quest? If all is Mind, is not Knowledge the highest attainment, the ability to understand and see the workings and unfoldment of everything?

David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Kybalion is among other things a great place to study the strengths as well as the weaknesses of the New Thought movement. The principle that "all is Mind" -- equating mind here with the thinking process, the intellectual side of human mental activity -- is really useful as a way of helping you figure out the pervasive role of thinking in our lives, and in particular in learning that most human problems come about because our thinking is screwed up in one way or another. On the other hand, if you equate Mind with thinking, and try to think your way to enlightenment, you're going to run face first into a brick wall. It's been tried, and it doesn't work.

As an old-fashioned ceremonial magician, I'd suggest a view that differs rather sharply from that of the Kybalion. Mind is not the All; mind is derivative from a far more basic reality. All is will; existence (the will to be) is the first and most basic mode of will, life (the will to live) is the second mode, and mind (the will to know) the third mode. Is there a fourth mode? You bet, and its name and nature are left as a theme for meditation... ;-)

comments.title: Geomancy question
comments.content:
Goats and Roses

Recently I did a geomancy chart with the question “What area of my life, if focused on for the next 1 to 2 months, will yield the most fruitful progress in my life's purpose?” Is this an appropriate question to get a clear answer? If it is not, then I won't bore you with the specifics of the chart. If this is a useful question, though, I will follow up asking a more detailed question. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy question
replies.content:
Goats and Roses

I don't want to miss the 10 pm cutoff, so I am adding this here before your answer to my first question. Just ignore if needed!

Here is the chart I cast.
1: Aquisitio
2: Amissio
3: Rubeus
4: Via
5: Aquisitio
6: Puer
7: Aquisitio
8: Carcer
9: Via
10: Puer
11: Cauda Draconis
12: Fortuna Minor

Right witness Rubeus, Left witness Albus, Judge Conjunctio

A little context here. I am in a pretty big life transition, I have been a caregiver for a family member for over a decade. Now things are shifting and I can contemplate focusing on my needs a lot more. I need to rebuild my finances but also my sense of self, joy in life, etc. I am not sorry I devoted the time, but now it seems appropriate to be more me-focused. Hence the question. Questions I am asking myself are should I focus on getting a job and some stability? Or enjoy doing things I haven't been able to do for quite a while, maybe a bit of traveling? Should I move? Will I be more successful getting a job or starting a business? Is now a good time to be open to finding a partner?

My initial take was that aquisitio in houses 1 5 and 7 meant that the chart perfected for anything related to house 5 and 7. So go out and do things that light me up, exercise my creativity, be open to business opportunities and partnership in life and/or in a business. What do you think of that interpretation?

What do you think is the meaning of Via in 4 and 9, also Puer in 6 and 10? Are they significant or not, since the question I asked was for one area to focus one, not lots. Does anything else jump out as important nuance to the chart?

Thank you for any insight into this tangle of possibility.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy question
replies.content:
It's a valid question, and you got a very solid answer. Acquisitio is indeed the figure to watch, and it tells you that you should work on fifth and seventh house matters. The fifth house, btw, is also the house of sexuality, so that plus seventh house suggests that being open to finding a partner might well belong at the top of the list. Conjunctio as the judge also is kind of a broad hint in that direction!

Since Via and Puer don't relate to the first house directly, and your question didn't specify any other houses, they shouldn't be read. If you want to know about whether you should travel, or what have you, you'll need to cast additional readings.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Happy new year, and thanks as always for doing this!

In “The Druidry Handbook,” you describe a soul’s journey from Annwn to Abred to Gwynfydd... to what extent do you think this teaching has commonalities with Teilhard de Chardin’s ideas about spiritual evolution, the Cosmic Christ, and Omega Point?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hmm! The great difference is that Teilhard tried to immanentize the Eschaton -- that is, to force the mythic images of his tradition into a historical context, so that the Omega Point would occur somewhere in linear time. That's a common way to read Christian myths, of course, so he had some excuse for doing so. Druids don't play that game, though; we by and large know better than to confuse kairos with chronos, linear time with the Dreamtime. Gwynfydd isn't something that will come into existence at some point in the future; it's present right here and right now, waiting for each of us to reach the point at which we can perceive it -- the point at which we walk through the walls and into the Fire.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What a fantastic answer—thanks so much! A lot to chew on there...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John—

Perhaps, to put my previous question another way, how is it that Daath is not the apex of the supernatural tetrahedron, with Kether (consciousness, nwyfre), Chokmah (force, gwyar), and Binah (form, calas) making up the basal pedestal? Not that he is the best example of right livelihood, but didn’t Crowley suggest that very notion?

David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If Crowley suggested it, it's probably a good idea to avoid! Still, it's possible to make an abstract argument for just about any reorganization of the universe, but then we have to deal with the awkward realities of what works and what doesn't. Daath isn't a sephirah; it is, to be precise, not an emanation but a shadow -- not a reality in its own right but a secondhand reflection of a reality. Those who treat it as a reality end up confusing thinking with consciousness, and get an ideology in place of enlightenment.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Also, not a question but a note: checked out my horoscope on Astro.com, and, indeed, some of the intricacies (Saturn, Venus, and Mercury all in the 7th house, Venus-Uranus sextile) as far as I've made out from the website interpretations really do explain the bits of the standard-issue-Libra stuff that doesn't click with me (social, peacemaker, incomplete without a partner, etc). Interesting!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Is your Uranus in the 5th or the 9th?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 9th--I've got the moon in the first and everything else in 7th-10th. It's a very lopsided chart to look at!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's going to amplify the effect of the Moon in your chart; when one planet dominates one half of the chart like that, it's strengthened in effect. That Uranus placement's considered highly favorable for astrology, btw -- if you decide you want to take up the study of astrology, you'll likely become very good at it very quickly.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

Okay, that makes sense. The house Uranus is in shows where to look for unconventional, independent-minded, and rebellious attitudes. Mine's in the first, which is the classic position of the bona fide eccentric, the one for whom the road less traveled is always the route of choice; the fifth is of course the house of sexuality, among other things, and sextile Venus -- well, you can no doubt draw your own conclusions. ;-)

Unfortunately that's not as beneficial a placement for studying astrology as in th e9th!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Alas! But does fit very well with me. :) The Uranus/Neptune/Pluto stuff's been harder for me to interpret--classics have given me a fair idea of the Sun-Saturn range, but those three are newer--so it's neat to get an idea there. Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't consider Pluto a planet -- to my mind, it belongs with Ceres, Chiron, and other minor bodies. Uranus is the planet of individuality and Neptune the planet of wholeness -- thus, among other things, Uranus shows where you're eccentric, and Neptune shows where you're not in charge. (Things like drug abuse, alcoholism, and madness are Neptunian, but so is mysticism and spirituality.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
And Neptune's in my fifth, too, in fact! Along with Mars. I'm pretty much all fifth and third, in fact, with the Moon over in the 8th and Jupiter in the 4th, along with minor-body Pluto--the website interpretation of which, in fact, strikes me as not quite relevant to me, so that makes sense.

(I knew nothing of this when I ended up swearing to my particular patrons, but...communication and sex, yeah. Whoa.)

comments.title: ONA
comments.content:
I apologize if this has been asked before, but: Are you familiar with the Order of the Nine Angles, and if so, what are your thoughts on them?

I've been vaguely familiar with the existence of the ONA for a long time from the fringes of the metal and experimental music scenes, but only recently started researching them more thoroughly. Your recent talk on secret societies was synchronous with a growing interest in the group, and in many ways they seem analogous to the Thule Society. Do you think it's possible that they—or similar groups—are likely to have an influence on political life in the west as it becomes more unstable?

replies.title: Re: ONA
replies.content: Yes, I'm familiar with them, having researched them for my encyclopedia of secret societies. It's possible that they, or some similar group, might play a role equivalent to that of the Thule-Gesellschaft or the other Ariosophical groups that fed into it -- but I certainly hope not.

comments.title: Teaching children magic
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
At what age is it safe to start teaching children about magic? I assume natural magic would be a good place to start. Is there a book you would recommend to begin teaching children?

I must confess that I started by giving my two young God daughters semi-precious gems and explaining how they work and how to care for them. That was two years ago and I just gave them some more gems including cut open geodes.

The girls still spend a lot of time washing their gems in cold water and playing with them. The parents approved when I asked if I could give them such gifts. I hope I have not started too soon.
Maxine Rogers

replies.title: Re: Teaching children magic
replies.content: Natural magic is fine for children, even quite young ones. I don't know a good book on the subject for children; it would be very welcome if there were such a thing. I don't recommend teaching them any kind of ceremonial magic until they're past puberty.

replies.title: Re: Teaching children magic
replies.content:
Thanks very much.
Happy New Year (I hope) to you and Sara.
Maxine

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi John. I'm just trying to keep the different systems straight in my head, and keep the books you've written in proper order. I've got your Druidry Handbook as well as the Druid Magic Handbook. Somewhere I remember that the Circles of Power book can be used with these first two books but the Paths of Wisdom book you cannot as it goes with the Celtic Golden Dawn system? Have I got it correct, or have I mixed them up?

And are there any more you've written that would be tied in with the first two I listed if Circles of Power is not correct?

David, not by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, it's a bit more complex than that. My books of magical instruction divide up into three groups.

Group I: Paths of Wisdom, Circles of Power, and Learning Ritual Magic.

Group II: The Druidry Handbook, The Druid Magic Handbook, The Druid Grove Handbook, The Gnostic Celtic Church: a Manual and Liturgy, and The Dolmen Arch (which will be published next year).

Group III: The Celtic Golden Dawn, The Coelbren Alphabet, and several works currently in process.

Any of the books in a given group can be used together with any of the other books in that group. Books in one group cannot be used together with books in another group. So, no, you can't use Circles of Power together with The Druidry Handbook -- they're entirely different systems. Does that help at all?

comments.title: Learning Native American Languages
comments.content:
JMG,

A few Magic Mondays ago, you had raised the question about learning some Naragansett words. IIRC, it was Robert Mathiesen who gave you some wonderful ideas. If I may, I'd like to add a bit based on my experiences.

First, to recall a bit of my background. My wife of many years is a member of an interior Salish speaking nation. I'm also the guy who wrote something on your other board a few months back about some very negative experiences I had trying some Native magic without permission of either the local spirits or any Native practitioners.

That said, one easy approach would be to search through local history books and see if there are any Narragansett words in old books, journals, etc. This would be akin to learning the Chinook Jargon from any of the several old books published on that language. Nobody should have a problem with that.

Otherwise, learning even a few words of the local lingo is all about relationships. There are a few rituals I can do now, under certain settings, because the local spirits are getting to know me, they know who my wife is and that under certain events, certain rites need to be observed by her husband. So, I'm getting to know the local spirits. Relating to the local spirits is key.


Second, should you choose to speak to some Narragansett people about this, probably build a relationship first, well before asking. Over the years, I've gotten to know a local Salish speaker who teaches immersion courses in the language. Because we know each other, he will answer any language question I may have. It also helps that he has seen over the years my sensitivity to and participation in the local Native cultures. Were I just some guy asking about Salish language, I'd likely be given the cold shoulder.

So, to summarize, I would get to know the local land spirits and build relationships with the local Natives just to relate. Becoming known, and that you're not a threat but hold the culture in esteem, could open doors.

Hope that helps.

DJSpo

replies.title: Re: Learning Native American Languages
replies.content: Thanks for this. My plan is simply to find a decent grammar of the Algonkian languages -- Narragansett is close enough to Mohegan and Wampanoag that some linguists consider it a dialect rather than an independent language -- and get some basic facility with the structure and vocabulary of the language. I don't expect to go any further unless I end up interacting with local members of the tribe, and they encourage further interest.

replies.title: Re: Learning Native American Languages
replies.content:
Since last week I've poked around in the Native American languages of Rhode Island a bit more. There's a boundary I had overlooked that runs from the Atlantic up Narragansett Bay, up the Seekonk River and then up the Blackstone River. Narragansett was spoken to the west of that line, Wampanoag ("Massachusett") to its east. They are close enough to one another that, as JMG noted, they are considered by some linguists to be dialects of the same language. But don't try to tell that to any modern Narragansett and Wampanoag! Their ancestors were ancient enemies, and their descendants still remember.

[The land to the east of that line, until you get fairly far upriver, originally belonged to Plymouth Colony, and then to Massachusetts, not to Rhode Island. It became part of Rhode Island only in the mid-1700s.]

For Narragansett we are largely dependent on Roger William's book I cited last week, but for Massachusett (Wampanoag) we have far better 17th-century sources, including John Eliot's full grammar of the language and his translation of the entire Bible (printed in 1663). Eliot's grammar is "The Indian Grammar Begun" (1666). Eliot also published a sort of conversation-book for the language, "Indian Dialogues" (1671).

The grammar can be downloaded here as a PDF:

archive.org/details/indiangrammarbeg00elio/page/n9

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In the old house we are currently renting, we are starting to have problems with mice. We have sealed up our food in jars and containers, tried to tape up holes in the walls, etc, and have caught a couple in live traps and released them outside. Are there karmic consequences to killing them in the most humane way possible (I suppose that would be snap traps)? How would a Druid deal with this problem?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I’m not a Druid, but I suggest not releasing them outside, they’ll just come back in.

Also, if you can, get a cat. Even if it’s 15 years old and sleeps all the time, its scent will help scare off the mice. And if you can find an enthusiastic mouser, grab it!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Avoid poisons, since those can concentrate up the food chain and harm other living things. Snap traps are probably a good choice, as is a cat. Releasing them outside won't do a thing -- they got into your house from outside, right? They'll just come back.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG, and a happy secular New Year to you & yours.

I'm planning on having a quiet evening racking mead and cooking a batch of lentils while the partner is out on a gig.

Peladan's "How to Become a Mage" - How exciting! Once released, where might we purchase that translation?

Ok, my question:

Could you speak a little about the "great voice"? (if that's the correct term) There are frequently times when I would like to perform ritual, but doing it out full vibratory voice would not be a prudent option. Do you simply imagine yourself vibrating the names as potently as possible in your head/whole body/radiating out into a place in space, or is there actual vocal cord movement involved?

Thanks!
Bonnie

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I move my mouth as though I was speaking aloud, and then imagine the sensation of vibration and the sound. It takes a little while to get the trick of Great Voice, but once you do, it's nearly as powerful as vibrating aloud.

The Peladan translation's going to be published by Llewellyn, of all people. I'll post something here when it comes out.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: If you asked if someone was lying and got Puella in the 1st house, Conjunctio in the 12th house, and no signs of perfection, how would you interpret it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The answer is no. Puella's a stable sign, therefore truthful, and since the chart doesn't perfect, the person isn't lying.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG

What do you make of the psychedelic renaissance and have you ever tried psychedelic drugs, magic mushrooms etc..

Also, how do you rate the Thoth deck compared to the Rider Waite?

Happy New Year too!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I dabbled in psychedelics during my first pass through college, including some fairly high doses of LSD. I found it uninteresting compared to the places I could get through magical practice, and stopped.

2) Neither of them really does that much for me, but the Rider-Waite deck at least isn't butt-ugly.

comments.title: Planes Below Ours
comments.content:
JMG,

When demons came up, you mentioned that they came from the plane below ours. What is that plane like?

I'll add a bit of context to hopefully make what I'm curious about clearer. The planes are often described in terms of density, but what does that actually mean? Alan Moore's Promethea includes a fictional depiction of Geotic demons where a character states that physical matter will burst apart if touched by a demon--a consequence of less dense material reacting to more dense material. Since missing persons aren't commonly associated with poorly drawn magic circles, I assume that's just one of the fictional liberties that Moore took, but it does make me realize that I have no idea what "density" refers to here: In the physical world, something being more dense just means it's heavier for a given volume. So, what does "dense" mean in the context of planes of existence? If things were just really heavy there, it would seem like not much would happen.

Or, if the whole question of density is off the mark, is there a better way to try to understand what a lower plane would be?

Thank you, and have a happy new year!

replies.title: Re: Planes Below Ours
replies.content: We don't know much about the planes below ours. "Density" needs to be understood as a metaphor -- and yes, Moore was dolling things up for dramatic effect. Think of the difference between air and water, and between water and earth; the idea is that the demonic plane is even more rigid and restricting than solid matter.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Hope you have a nice New Years Eve! I had three particular questions:

1. I just planted an herb garden outside my apartment, which I am very excited about--I cook a great deal and I was hoping to harvest some of the herbs for use in magical work. I also finally picked up your Encyclopedia of Natural Magic. In it you mentioned not to use steel to cut the herbs because it will dissipate the magical qualities. This makes sense, but, being new to thinking magically, I hadn't thought of that before harvesting a bit of rosemary last night with stainless steel scissors. How strong is that banishing effect? Should this particular plant be used wholly for cookery now? Does that magical essence return over time?

2. I posted a question in the AODA forum about magical protection the other day and thought I'd get your view. From what I've read (and from what I've seen you say) beginning magical practice can light you up on the astral plane, potentially attracting some less than desirable spiritual beings. From what I've read, the LBRP certainly does this, though it does provide banishing as well. Even reading today on the AODA website, the SoP was formulated originally by the Magickal Order of the Golden Dawn, presumably by Dr. Ashley. This makes me think that the SoP would do the same kind of a thing astrally. I'm only just beginning with daily SoP, so I'm not sure to what degree this should be a concern at all. What are the similarities/differences between the LBRP and the SoP in how they function/effects they produce?

3. Related to the previous question, someone on Reddit suggested that I add the Rose Cross ritual to my regular practice. I don't intend to meddle with the Golden Dawn stuff until I feel I have really grasped everything in the DMH and supplemental AODA material like the Dolmen Arch (very excited about that book coming out), which means quite a few years since I'm only just beginning. Is the SoP sufficient for these purposes? Is there a Rose Cross equivalent that is compatible with the system of AODA? What might you suggest I do to provide some additional magical protection? I smudge my apartment fairly regularly, I'm in the process of making my own Florida Water and some other magical waters to add to floor washes, etc.

Thank you, as always, for all of your advice. I love reading through all the questions and am very grateful you make yourself available in this way to aspirants on the path!

David

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It just dissipates the magical qualities of the piece you cut off. The rest of the plant is fine.

2) The SoP is a very different ritual, and it has different effects. In particular, it doesn't have the lighting-up effect of the LBRP, so don't worry about it.

3) No, the R+C ritual isn't compatible with the DMH/Dolmen Arch work, but then you don't need it to be. The SoP will do everything you need -- that's why I gave it in the DMH as the core ritual practice. Yes, you can also use natural magic to help out, but as you pick up some skill with the SoP, you won't really need it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You can often find very sharp stone knives on ebay. I have one with a stone handle as well as blade, for magical use. The process of making such a stone blade is called "knapping"; putting some form of that word in the search engine will halp narrow down the possibilities.

Mostly they're made for sale to collectors, but if you buy one from the maker, he will know how to knapp it sharp enough for real use in your garden.

comments.title: Telekinesis
comments.content:
What's your take on telekinesis? Do you think it's possible for someone to move a physical object "with their mind"? If so, what is your understanding of the mechanism?

I ask because in a previous Magic Monday (I haven't found the reference) a commenter asked about an experience they had had where a book on a shelf moved as they were reaching for it, and you described the experience as "psychokinesis."

Tangentially, as my palm centers have been awakening, I've had the urge as I reach for things to "just move them telekinetically." It took me a while to try it, but nothing happened. Which is just as well. As Catherine MacCoun put it: " To succeed would be impressive, but also pointless. To move objects is why God gave you hands."

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Telekinesis
replies.content: I've never seen anyone who could do it, so I've assigned it the Scots verdict of "not proven." Books falling off shelves -- well, it's not as though human beings are the only things that can move physical objects, and while we need hands, that's not necessarily true of other beings...

replies.title: Re: Telekinesis
replies.content: If you want to try moving things via telekinesis, you might try influencing a candle flame to move toward or away from you, or getting the smoke from incense to move in a particular direction. Or focus on the air immediately surrounding the flame or the smoke and try to create a current in that.

comments.title: Natural Magic Timing
comments.content:
A question about timing for natural magic. When using the planetary hours method of timing the creation of natural magic items (or any of the methods, really), what is the specific point which determines the timing?

In this case, my wife made some soap and we are going to use it as a base to make a magically infused soap. To finish the soap and add the items we need to grate it, slowly melt it, then add our items, and then re-pour it into molds. All this may take more than a planetary hour so, is there a specific point which would be the "creation" or does all of this have to take place in the target planetary hour?

Thanks and enjoy your new year celebrations!

HV

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic Timing
replies.content: What matters is when you begin. Just as a horoscope is drawn up for the moment of birth, the moment of beginning a process is the one that's magically significant in terms of timing.

comments.title: Scrying
comments.content:
John Michael,
I have been doing the complete Sphere of Protection ritual daily as described in the DMH since mid-September. I haven’t done any deliberate magic, just the ritual as described plus opening and closing a Grove to celebrate the three Druid holy days that have occurred since then. As a next step, I am feeling called to start scrying in the Druid Grove, journeying through the gates of the Elements. Do you recommend using a scrying mirror placed in the center of the altar for this, or do you maintain your gaze in the direction of the gate being scryed?

And a second question, I have an obsidian scrying mirror. What are your thoughts on using obsidian for scrying? I understand that obsidian is related to energies of Saturn which might be good or perhaps not. (I have considerable Saturn influence in my birth chart.)
Thank you as always, and have a joyful New Year’s celebration.

Yanocoches
P.S. Thank you for the picture of Joséphin Péladan. Did you ever make it to the Peladan exhibition that was going on (in New York, if I remember correctly) about the time you moved to Providence? He sounds like a fascinating individual, not unlike you is what I thought when you first spoke about him on the old blog.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content:
I don't use a scrying mirror, having no particular talent for that form of scrying. I do what's called scrying in the spirit vision, which is done with the imagination rather than with a mirror, crystal ball, etc. You'll probably want to experiment and see what works best for you.

If you live in the Southwest, I don't recommend the use of obsidian scrying mirrors unless you're comfortable working with Aztec deities. The obsidian mirror was the symbol of Tezcatlipoca, the god of darkness and great enemy of Quetzalcoatl, and that egregor is far from dead.

No, unfortunately, I never managed to make it down to the exhibit -- too much else to do.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have found the Cosmic Doctrine already incredibly useful and it's made me want to attempt to learn to use ritual magic, I have your book on the subject, Learning Ritual Magic, which is what I intended to use. I have read a little of the beginning of it, enough to know that I need to make a trip to an occult shop to get a tarot deck and some of the books I need, but before I did that I wanted to ask you something. I have time early in the morning almost every day now, as I used to go in to work quite early but it isn't necessary these days, and may not be again for some time, which gives me ample time, and I want to do the work, but this would be at home with my family. That includes one young child under 2 and a soon to be born baby - is what you describe in that book safe to do around young children? They would be asleep most likely, and on a different floor of our house. Are there things you would advise? Forgive me if this is a foolish question, I am just not sure what I am embarking on here and would rather be safe than sorry.

Thank you, and thanks again for introducing me to Dion Fortune's writing and going through the Cos Doc with us (I recently reread all of your articles so far in the series and found them very helpful - I couldn't fully make sense of them initially, although I read each a couple times when you posted them, because I hadn't worked through the steps of her writing and I found whatever insight I got from you was smashed apart when I'd try to read the text) Slowly working through this puzzle has shown me things about my thinking, and things I can do with thought that I did not know I could do before, and has me curious about taking this further.

Thanks again,
Johnny

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's not a foolish question at all.  With a very young child and a child on the way, meditation, divination, and natural magic are fine, but I don't recommend magical ritual until your children are past their third birthday.  Put the time into reading, study, and the other practices just mentioned, and when you can take up magic you'll be prepared to get excellent results.

comments.title: More depth?
comments.content:
Hiya Boss
While my copies of your Weird novels have not arrived from Founders House order dated 12/7 yet, I am curious about the origins of Lovecraft’s mythos and subsequent development. Are there any titles you recommend for those of us who want to dig a bit deeper? After being knocked out of the intelligentsia by economic caprice and discovering the ink on my New England gentry pedigree is still wet, I have nothing to prevent me from exploring the all the wrong sort of questions with all the wrong sort of people… and – about that magic system for fans of the Cthulhu Mythos… I could be interested…
In the department of TSW – The sudden appearance of a Lakeland Republic flag in a mysterious package from Oz (delivered by a tornado, of course) has touched off a bit of strangeness here. To wit: singing “O, Lakeland, my Lakeland shine on!” to the tune of “Hang on Sloopy.” That’s not gonna fly with the other states, but it made for an odd feeling that the future is a real place. Are we just amateur Green Wizards overcome by the heady vapors of off-brand soda pop, or is something happening?
Thanks!
Rusty

replies.title: Re: More depth?
replies.content:
1) If you haven't gotten your copies yet, contact the publisher and find out if there's been some kind of problem. You should have gotten them a while ago.

2) Start by reading Lovecraft's own stories, and the collection Tales of the Cthulhu Mythos, which is a good collection of Mythos stories in general. There are several bios of Lovecraft, and one of those might be worth taking in. Oh, and S.T. Joshi's H.P. Lovecraft: The Decline of the West, which relates Lovecraft's work to Oswald Spengler, is very much worth reading.

3) That's quite some soda pop! I don't know; stranger things have happened...

comments.title: Lakeland National Anthem
comments.content:
The Star-Spangled Banner is sung to a variant of the tune of "To Anacreon In Heaven" which I understand to have been the drinking song of an upper class men's club. So you're on the right track.

I noticed that the anthem at the end of Through the Looking Glass, "To the Looking Glass World It Was Alice Who Said", seems to be a filk of "To Anacreon In Heaven", and it can also be sung to the tune of The Star-Spangled Banner.
